quiz_questions.question_text: What does Hooke's Law describe?

options.text: The relationship between spring force and extension
The relationship between mass and gravitational force
The relationship between friction and surface area
The relationship between pressure and temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law states that the force exerted by a spring is directly proportional to its extension or compression, as long as the elastic limit is not exceeded. The formula is F = kx.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the formula F = kx, what does 'k' represent?

options.text: The mass of the object on the spring
The spring constant (stiffness of the spring)
The length of the entire spring at rest
The gravitational acceleration at that spot

quiz_questions.explanation: In F = kx, 'k' is the spring constant, which measures the stiffness of the spring. A larger k means a stiffer spring that requires more force to stretch or compress by the same amount.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the formula F = kx, what does 'x' represent?

options.text: The original length of the spring at rest
The mass hanging from the spring below
The extension or compression of the spring
The speed of the spring when it bounces

quiz_questions.explanation: In F = kx, 'x' is the displacement of the spring from its natural (resting) length. It can be either an extension (stretching) or a compression (squeezing), measured in meters.

quiz_questions.question_text: What unit is the spring constant (k) measured in?

options.text: Kilograms per meter
Meters per second squared
Newtons per meter (N/m)
Joules per kilogram value

quiz_questions.explanation: The spring constant k is measured in newtons per meter (N/m). It tells you how many newtons of force are needed to stretch or compress the spring by one meter.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you double the extension of a spring (within elastic limit), what happens to the force?

options.text: The force doubles as well for that spring
The force stays the same as before always
The force is cut in half from before
The force quadruples from the original value

quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law (F = kx) shows a direct proportion: if you double x, the force F also doubles. This linear relationship holds as long as the spring is not stretched past its elastic limit.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the elastic limit of a spring?

options.text: The point at which the spring cannot return to its original shape
The maximum speed the spring can reach when released from rest
The resting length of the spring when no force is applied to it
The temperature at which the spring melts into liquid metal form

quiz_questions.explanation: The elastic limit is the maximum extension or compression a spring can undergo while still returning to its original shape. Beyond this point, the spring is permanently deformed and Hooke's Law no longer applies.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spring with k = 100 N/m is stretched 0.2 m. What force does it exert?

options.text: 500 newtons of force
20 newtons of force
0.002 newtons of force
200 newtons of force

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = kx: F = 100 N/m x 0.2 m = 20 N. The spring exerts a restoring force of 20 newtons, pulling back toward its natural length.

quiz_questions.question_text: What direction does the spring force act?

options.text: In the same direction as the stretch or compression
Always pointing straight downward toward the Earth
Opposite to the direction of stretch or compression
In a random direction each time it is stretched

quiz_questions.explanation: The spring force (restoring force) always acts opposite to the direction of displacement. If you stretch a spring to the right, it pulls back to the left. If compressed, it pushes back outward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which spring is stiffer: one with k = 50 N/m or one with k = 200 N/m?

options.text: The spring with k = 50 N/m is stiffer
Both springs are equally stiff overall
The spring with k = 200 N/m is stiffer
Stiffness cannot be determined from k

quiz_questions.explanation: A higher spring constant means a stiffer spring. k = 200 N/m requires 200 N to stretch it by 1 meter, while k = 50 N/m only needs 50 N. The 200 N/m spring is four times stiffer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can Hooke's Law apply to compressing a spring as well as stretching it?

options.text: No, it only applies to stretching springs out
Yes, it applies to both stretching and compressing
No, compressed springs follow different laws
Yes, but only for very large compressions here

quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law applies equally to both extension (stretching) and compression (squeezing) of a spring, as long as the elastic limit is not exceeded. F = kx works for both cases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Hooke's Law?

options.text: The law stating spring force is proportional to displacement
The law stating objects at rest stay at rest without force
The law stating every action has an equal opposite reaction
The law stating energy cannot be created or destroyed ever

quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law states that the force needed to extend or compress a spring is directly proportional to the distance of extension or compression. The formula F = kx expresses this relationship mathematically.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'spring constant' mean?

options.text: A measure of how stiff or resistant a spring is to deformation
The natural resting length of a spring with no force applied
The maximum weight a spring can hold before breaking apart
The speed at which a spring bounces back to original shape

quiz_questions.explanation: The spring constant (k) measures the stiffness of a spring. A higher k value means the spring is harder to stretch or compress. It is measured in newtons per meter (N/m).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'elastic' mean in the context of springs?

options.text: Able to return to original shape after being deformed
Permanently changed in shape after being deformed
Unable to be stretched or compressed at all here
Made of rubber material only for this purpose

quiz_questions.explanation: Elastic means the material can return to its original shape after the force is removed. Springs are elastic within their elastic limit. Beyond that, they become permanently deformed (plastic deformation).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'restoring force' mean?

options.text: The force that pushes a spring back to its natural length
The force that stretches a spring beyond its elastic limit
The gravitational force acting on the spring and mass
The friction force between the spring and the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The restoring force is the force a spring exerts to return to its equilibrium (natural) position. It always points toward the equilibrium, opposing the displacement that caused it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'extension' mean for a spring?

options.text: How much longer the spring is compared to its natural length
The total length of the spring when nothing is attached
The maximum force the spring can exert when stretched
The stiffness of the spring measured in newtons per meter

quiz_questions.explanation: Extension is the amount a spring has been stretched beyond its natural (resting) length. If a spring's natural length is 10 cm and it is stretched to 15 cm, the extension is 5 cm.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'compression' mean for a spring?

options.text: Stretching the spring longer than its natural length
Squeezing the spring shorter than its natural length
Breaking the spring into two separate pieces
Keeping the spring at exactly its resting length

quiz_questions.explanation: Compression is when a spring is pushed shorter than its natural length. The compressed spring pushes back outward with a restoring force proportional to the amount of compression.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'equilibrium position' for a spring?

options.text: The natural resting position where the spring exerts no force
The maximum stretch point where force is greatest on it
The position where the spring is fully compressed down
The point where the spring breaks apart permanently

quiz_questions.explanation: The equilibrium position is where the spring is at its natural length and exerts no force. When displaced from equilibrium, the restoring force pulls or pushes the spring back toward this position.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'directly proportional' mean in Hooke's Law?

options.text: As one quantity doubles the other one doubles too
As one quantity doubles the other is cut in half
Both quantities always remain exactly the same
The two quantities have no relationship at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Directly proportional means when one quantity increases, the other increases by the same factor. In F = kx, if x doubles, F doubles. If x triples, F triples. They change together in the same ratio.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'deformation' mean?

options.text: A change in the shape or size of an object due to force
The natural resting shape of an object with no forces
The color change that occurs when stretching a spring
The temperature increase in a spring during stretching

quiz_questions.explanation: Deformation is a change in the shape or size of an object caused by an applied force. Elastic deformation is temporary (object returns to shape), while plastic deformation is permanent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'elastic potential energy' mean?

options.text: Energy stored in a stretched or compressed spring
Energy of a moving object due to its velocity
Energy stored due to an object's height above ground
Energy transferred as heat between two objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy is the energy stored in a spring (or any elastic object) when it is stretched or compressed. When released, this stored energy can be converted into kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spring with k = 50 N/m is stretched 0.1 m. What force does it exert?

options.text: 500 newtons of restoring force
5 newtons of restoring force
0.5 newtons of restoring force
50 newtons of restoring force

quiz_questions.explanation: F = kx = 50 N/m x 0.1 m = 5 N. The spring exerts a 5 newton restoring force opposite to the direction it was stretched.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bungee cord stretches 4 m when a 600 N person hangs from it at rest. What is the spring constant?

options.text: 150 N/m for the bungee cord
2400 N/m for the bungee cord
600 N/m for the bungee cord
4 N/m for the bungee cord

quiz_questions.explanation: At rest, the spring force balances the weight: F = kx, so k = F/x = 600 N / 4 m = 150 N/m. The bungee cord has a spring constant of 150 newtons per meter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student hangs weights on a spring and measures the extension. The graph of force vs. extension is a straight line. What does this confirm?

options.text: The spring does not obey Hooke's Law at all
The spring has been stretched past its elastic limit
The spring obeys Hooke's Law within this range
The spring has no spring constant whatsoever

quiz_questions.explanation: A straight line on a force vs. extension graph confirms Hooke's Law (F = kx). The linear relationship means force is directly proportional to extension. The slope of the line equals the spring constant k.

quiz_questions.question_text: A toy uses a compressed spring to launch a ball. What energy conversion occurs when the spring is released?

options.text: Kinetic energy converts to gravitational potential energy
Thermal energy converts to elastic potential energy
Gravitational potential energy converts into thermal energy
Elastic potential energy converts to kinetic energy

quiz_questions.explanation: The compressed spring stores elastic potential energy. When released, this energy converts to kinetic energy of the ball, launching it into the air. The stiffer the spring and greater the compression, the more energy released.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mattress spring has k = 1000 N/m and another has k = 300 N/m. Which feels softer to lie on?

options.text: The 1000 N/m spring feels much softer overall
The 300 N/m spring feels softer because it is less stiff
Both springs feel exactly the same to lie on
Neither spring can be used in a mattress at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The 300 N/m spring is less stiff, meaning it compresses more easily under your weight. This makes it feel softer. The 1000 N/m spring resists compression more and feels firmer.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rubber band is stretched to twice its length. When released, what causes it to snap back?

options.text: The restoring force pulling it back to natural length
Gravity pulling the rubber band back downward here
Air resistance pushing the rubber band to contract
Friction between the rubber and the air around it

quiz_questions.explanation: The stretched rubber band has a restoring force that pulls it back toward its natural length. This follows Hooke's Law (approximately). The stored elastic potential energy converts to kinetic energy as it snaps back.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spring scale reads 30 N when a fish is hung from it, stretching the spring 0.15 m. What is the spring constant?

options.text: 4.5 N/m for the spring scale
200 N/m for the spring scale
0.005 N/m for the spring scale
30 N/m for the spring scale

quiz_questions.explanation: k = F/x = 30 N / 0.15 m = 200 N/m. The spring scale has a spring constant of 200 N/m. Spring scales work by using Hooke's Law to measure weight from extension.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car's suspension spring is compressed 0.05 m when a 75 kg person sits in the car. What is the approximate spring constant (g = 10 m/s squared)?

options.text: 150 N/m for the spring
750 N/m for the spring
15000 N/m for the spring
37.5 N/m for the spring

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mg = 75 x 10 = 750 N. Using k = F/x = 750 N / 0.05 m = 15000 N/m. Car suspension springs are very stiff (high k) to support heavy loads without compressing too much.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student stretches a spring with 10 N (extends 2 cm), then 20 N (extends 4 cm), then 30 N (extends 6 cm). What pattern does this show?

options.text: Force and extension are inversely proportional here
There is no pattern at all in this data set here
Force and extension are directly proportional (Hooke's Law)
Extension decreases as force increases over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Each time the force doubles, the extension doubles (10 N gives 2 cm, 20 N gives 4 cm, 30 N gives 6 cm). This direct proportionality confirms Hooke's Law: F = kx with k = 500 N/m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A trampoline has springs with a combined spring constant of 5000 N/m. A child weighing 400 N stands on it. How much do the springs compress?

options.text: 0.08 meters of compression
12.5 meters of compression
2000 meters of compression
0.8 meters of compression

quiz_questions.explanation: Using F = kx, we solve for x: x = F/k = 400 N / 5000 N/m = 0.08 m (8 cm). The trampoline springs compress 8 centimeters under the child's weight.
